explanation: The requirement states that the user should be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. The atomic propositions available are 'enableLeakCompensation' (indicates user enables leak compensation) and, though there is no explicit 'disableLeakCompensation', the lack of enabling (i.e., not enableLeakCompensation) would correspond to disabling. Since the property emphasizes the user's freedom to change this setting at any time, the best match is to require that always (globally), if the user chooses to enable leak compensation, then it is enabled, and if not, then it is not; however, the atomic proposition only captures the act of enabling. Thus, the LTL property should reflect that it is always possible to enable the feature, whenever the user chooses. Since the template library does not allow arbitrary switching between two states based only on a single atomic proposition, the closest fit is to require that at any time, if the user enables leak compensation, then the system reflects this action. This is captured by: 'G (enableLeakCompensation)', indicating it's always possible (not forbidden) and can happen at any time upon user request. However, since the property is mostly granting permission, the atomic will not restrict when this happens, but simply allow it at any time. If we consider the act of enabling/leak compensation as an event, then 'F (enableLeakCompensation)' (eventually, at any time, this can be done) does not capture the repeated possibility, whereas 'G (True)' is trivial. Therefore, the best template to capture this 'at any time' is 'G (bool_exp3)', with 'bool_exp3 = enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation' (i.e., true). Given the template list, the best fit actually is leaving the system unconstrained from any time restriction, accepting that this is a system permission and always possible. Therefore, using 'G (True)' is trivial but matches the unconstrained property. Alternatively, to represent the ability to toggle at any time, you would need more atomic propositions (e.g., enable and disable).

The requirement states that the user must be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. This means that, at any point during operation, it should be possible for the user to activate or deactivate leak compensation. Given the atomic propositions, 'enableLeakCompensation' directly captures the user's action of enabling (and, by toggling, also disabling) the feature. To reflect the 'at any time' ability, the temporal property should allow this action to occur at any point in the timeline. The most direct way to express this freedom is to require that 'enableLeakCompensation' is always possible, i.e., that there is no temporal restriction that prevents it from occurring. In LTL, this can be expressed by 'G (true)', but to make the property specific to the user action, we capture that 'enableLeakCompensation' can occur at any time with 'F (enableLeakCompensation)' holding in any future (which, in practice, just means it is always possible to reach a state where the user can enable/disable the feature). However, since the requirement concerns being able to do the action any time, the best expression is to state that 'enableLeakCompensation' is always allowed to be changed, i.e., at every time step, it could be toggled. This is best captured with 'G (true)', but we want to be specific: at any point in time, it is possible for the user to set 'enableLeakCompensation', which is best modeled as 'G true' (trivial) or 'G (enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation)', but both branches are tautologies. Given the available atomic proposition, the closest formalization is: globally, at any future point, the user can cause 'enableLeakCompensation'. Thus, the template 'F bool_exp3' is appropriate, where bool_exp3 = enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation, which is always true. Alternatively, if we use '(F[0,N_DURATION] (enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation))', but to be specific, just 'G true' is vacuous. Given the context, the intent is captured by 'always possible to do the action' but, as an LTL requirement on the system, a more meaningful property is to say: at any time, the user can initiate enabling or disabling leak compensation. Thus, for every time point, no matter the state, the user can take the action to set 'enableLeakCompensation'. Given the constraints, the template 'G true' is too vacuous, so the next best is 'G (true)' but, for this AP, to say 'enableLeakCompensation' can be happened at any time, so using 'G (true)' is the best, but not in your template list. Therefore, mapping to available templates, we can use 'G bool_exp3' with 'bool_exp3 = (enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation)' which is always true, but as an LTL expression in this context, it conveys that no restriction is placed on 'enableLeakCompensation'.

The requirement states the user shall be able to enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time. From the list of atomic propositions, 'enableLeakCompensation' maps to enabling leak compensation, but there is no explicit atomic proposition representing 'disable'. However, enabling and disabling can be controlled with the Boolean expressions 'enableLeakCompensation' (for enable action) and '!enableLeakCompensation' (for disable action). Since the requirement is that the user can do this at any time, it means there is no restriction at any moment in the system operation for asserting or negating 'enableLeakCompensation'. This is best captured at the atomic/action level with the LTL property that states: it is always possible in the future to perform 'enable' (i.e., the system allows it at any time), and likewise for 'disable'. This aligns with 'G(F bool_exp3)' for possibility, but given the templates, the closest match is to use 'G (true)' as a trivial property or, more concretely, to formalize the absence of restriction: that at any time, enabling or disabling can be performed. Among the provided templates, the template 'F bool_exp3' best captures the idea that at any time, enable or disable can eventually be performed. Since both need to always be possible, we take their future possibility as an invariant: G(F enableLeakCompensation) and G(F !enableLeakCompensation). However, with the provided templates, option 4 ('F[0,N_DURATION] bool_exp3') can be used to state that within a bounded time, enabling or disabling can always occur. Here, to denote both actions, we use the Boolean expression '(enableLeakCompensation | !enableLeakCompensation)', which is always true, but it expresses the lack of system restriction for both enabling and disabling at any time. Setting N_DURATION = 10 as a reasonable bound.

The requirement is that the user can enable or disable the leak compensation feature at any time, which means there are no restrictions at any point in time preventing enable or disable. Since there are no atomic propositions representing prohibitive or blocking conditions, the best possible interpretation is that there must not be any restriction on 'enableLeakCompensation' at any time. The user shall be able to disable/enable the leak compensation feature at any time means that, throughout the operation of the system, the user (operator) can activate (enableLeakCompensation) or deactivate it (possibly by not asserting enableLeakCompensation) at any point (i.e., there are no restrictions or specific conditions in which it is allowed/disallowed). Since the proposition 'enableLeakCompensation' refers to the user enabling the leak compensation, and the requirement does not forbid any change at any moment, it is sufficient to say that, at all times, it is possible (eventually) to enable it—a liveness property. However, since this is a functionality under total user control (and there are no restrictions or relationships with other APs), the simplest formalization for 'the user can enable/disable at any time' is to state that, globally, it is always possible for this feature to be enabled (or disabled), i.e., there is no state where it is forbidden. Thus, the most appropriate LTL property is 'Globally, true' (i.e., 'G true'), but since the template list requires an AP, the next closest representation is to use 'G' with the atomic proposition directly.
